title: Cat Hair Loss Around Eyes: Causes, Urgency, and Triage Tips
meta_desc: Find out why your cat has hair loss around their eyes from rubbing, the urgency level, and how to help them feel better with our triage guide.
content_html:
TL;DR: Hair loss around a cat's eyes is often caused by irritation or allergies and typically requires a vet visit within 24 to 48 hours. Immediate care is needed if you notice squinting, swelling, or yellow discharge, as persistent rubbing can lead to serious eye injuries.

Why is my cat losing hair around their eyes?

Hair loss around the eyes, medically referred to as periocular alopecia, is a common sign that your cat is experiencing discomfort. Common causes include:

  • Environmental or food allergies
  • Parasitic infections such as mites
  • Fungal issues like ringworm
  • Persistent rubbing in response to underlying itch or irritation

Is hair loss around my cat's eyes a veterinary emergency?

The urgency level for hair loss around the eyes from rubbing is classified as Medium. While hair loss is rarely life-threatening, prompt action is required because:

  • Rubbing can lead to corneal ulcers or secondary bacterial infections if the eye surface is scratched.
  • A veterinary appointment should be scheduled within the next 24 to 48 hours.
  • You should seek immediate care if your cat is squinting, holding the eye shut, or exhibiting significant swelling and yellow discharge.

How can taking a photo of my cat's eyes help the veterinarian?

Providing a high-quality photograph of your cat’s eyes is a vital tool for triage. A clear photo allows a veterinary professional to assess:

  • The specific distribution and pattern of the hair loss
  • The color and health of the underlying skin
  • Any visible inflammation of the conjunctiva
  • Whether the problem is localized to the skin or if the eye itself is the primary source of pain

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

In cats presenting with hair loss around the eyes, acquired alopecia can be focal, multifocal, symmetric, or generalized, and is often influenced by an underlying cause, most commonly a pruritic disease like flea allergy dermatitis. Inflammatory changes may include hyperpigmentation, lichenification, erythema, scaling, excessive shedding, and pruritus. Other differentials to consider include infectious skin diseases (bacterial pyoderma, dermatophytosis), ectoparasites, allergic skin diseases (atopic dermatitis, food allergy, contact, insect hypersensitivity), and less commonly, neoplastic skin diseases. Marked hair loss can also occur after respiratory infection. For cats without obvious flea infestations, a CBC with differential is recommended to evaluate for eosinophilia, suggestive of flea allergy dermatitis.

Chapter: Dermatology

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 842)

faqs.question: Can allergies cause my cat to lose hair around the eyes?
faqs.answer: Yes, allergies to pollen, dust, or specific proteins in food can cause intense itching, leading your cat to rub their face and lose hair.

faqs.question: Should I try to clean the area with human eye wash?
faqs.answer: It is best to avoid human medications or washes unless specifically directed by a vet, as some ingredients can be toxic or irritating to feline eyes.

faqs.question: Will the hair eventually grow back?
faqs.answer: In most cases, yes. Once the underlying cause of the irritation is treated and the rubbing stops, the hair follicles usually recover and the fur returns.
